frontier.name: Alzheimer's drug-target landscape
frontier.description: Curated frontier of the five major Alzheimer's drug targets (Aβ, BACE1, Tau, TREM2, ApoE), bounded to one curator's synthesis of the literature. Each finding is an evergreen declarative claim from a working zettelkasten; links record the contradictions and supports the curator sees in the field. v0.11 republish: structured provenance (DOI, year, journal, source-authors) and conditions (scope text, species, study-type) populated for every finding.

assertion.text: Amyloid cascade hypothesis remains dominant despite clinical failures

conditions.text: Field-level synthesis covering 2003-2016 amyloid trial outcomes; framing applies to late-onset sporadic AD; does not extend cleanly to autosomal-dominant familial AD pedigrees where the cascade may operate differently.

provenance.title: Selkoe & Hardy, 2016, EMBO Mol Med 8:595-608

annotations.text: Curator open question: is the right therapeutic strategy to target one pathway, or to combine across amyloid + tau + microglial? The 'gravity well' nature of cascade-thinking biases toward single-pathway intervention design even when the evidence supports multifactorial models.
annotations.author: reviewer:will-blair

assertion.text: Lecanemab showed modest but real efficacy in the Clarity AD trial

conditions.text: Patients with mild cognitive impairment or mild AD dementia, ages 50-90, with confirmed amyloid PET positivity. Phase 3 RCT, 1795 patients, 18 months, biweekly IV infusion at 10 mg/kg. CDR-SB primary endpoint (mean difference -0.45 vs placebo, p<0.001). Modest absolute effect; 17.3% ARIA-E rate.

provenance.title: van Dyck et al., 2023, NEJM 388:9-21 (Clarity AD)

links.note: Lecanemab partial efficacy is the strongest recent vindication of the amyloid hypothesis

Lecanemab's clinical efficacy extends the case that anti-Aβ antibodies can reduce brain amyloid; BBB's TfR-shuttle work established the same principle preclinically via a different delivery route. Both target the brain-amyloid-clearance bottleneck.

assertion.text: Aβ plaque burden correlates poorly with cognitive decline

conditions.text: Cross-sectional and longitudinal autopsy and PET imaging studies; finding holds across multiple cohorts (ADNI, Mayo Clinic Study of Aging, Sun Health) but observational confounding by tau and other co-pathologies remains.

provenance.title: Jack et al., 2010, Lancet Neurol 9:119-128

links.note: Plaque-cognition decoupling is the central tension against the amyloid cascade as sole explanation

assertion.text: BACE1 inhibitor trials failed due to mechanism-independent side effects

conditions.text: EPOCH/APECS trials of verubecestat in mild-to-moderate AD and prodromal AD respectively. Trials terminated early for futility AND on-target adverse effects (worsening cognition, weight loss, neuropsychiatric symptoms). Same pattern across atabecestat (Janssen) and umibecestat (Novartis).

provenance.title: Egan et al., 2019, NEJM 380:1408-1420 (EPOCH verubecestat trial)

annotations.text: Curator open question: should the verubecestat/atabecestat failures map to confidence drift on the amyloid cascade itself? The trials failed for mechanism-independent reasons; the cleanest reading is that they should NOT be evidence against amyloid as a target. But the field treats them as such, which is itself a structural fact about how disconfirmation propagates in the evidence graph.
annotations.author: reviewer:will-blair

assertion.text: BACE1 has essential non-amyloid substrates that explain inhibitor toxicity

conditions.text: BACE1 substrates beyond APP include neuregulin-1 (myelination), Jagged-1/2 (Notch signaling), seizure protein 6, CHL1 (axon guidance). Conditional BACE1 knockouts show synaptic, myelination, and behavioral deficits. Implications generalize across BACE1 inhibitor classes.

provenance.title: Vassar et al., 2014, Nat Neurosci 17:1183-1191

links.note: Non-amyloid substrates explain the mechanism-independent toxicity that killed verubecestat

assertion.text: Tau pathology tracks cognitive decline more closely than amyloid

conditions.text: Longitudinal flortaucipir + Pittsburgh Compound B PET in 60 cognitively normal older adults followed up to 7 years. Tau accumulation in entorhinal cortex predicted cognitive decline; amyloid burden alone did not. Generalizes to clinically diagnosed mild AD; weaker fit for atypical variants.

provenance.title: Hanseeuw et al., 2019, JAMA Neurol 76:915-924

links.note: Tau-cognition coupling reinforces the case that amyloid is not the proximal driver of decline

assertion.text: Tau propagation follows predictable Braak staging patterns

conditions.text: Original autopsy series of 83 cases. Stages I-VI of neurofibrillary tangle distribution: transentorhinal → limbic → isocortical. Replicated in larger series and via PET tau imaging in vivo (Schöll et al., 2016). Some atypical AD presentations (PCA, lvPPA) deviate from the canonical sequence.

provenance.title: Braak & Braak, 1991, Acta Neuropathol 82:239-259

links.note: Braak staging gives tau a stronger progression logic than amyloid burden

assertion.text: TREM2 loss-of-function increases amyloid plaque density

conditions.text: TREM2 KO crossed onto APP/PS1, 5xFAD, and APPPS1-21 mouse models shows ~2-3x plaque density increase, especially dense-core plaques. Effect size depends on age (stronger >12mo) and brain region (cortex > hippocampus). Mechanism: failure of disease-associated microglia (DAM) state transition.

provenance.title: Gratuze et al., 2023, Nat Rev Neurol — TREM2 review

links.note: TREM2 LOF increasing plaques is consistent with amyloid being a real driver, just not the only one

annotations.text: Therapeutic implication is timing-dependent: activating microglia in late-stage disease where neurodegeneration is established may produce inflammatory harm. Activated microglia also release inflammatory cytokines. The 'when' of TREM2 activation matters as much as the 'what'.
annotations.author: reviewer:will-blair

assertion.text: TREM2 variants represent the strongest genetic risk after ApoE4

conditions.text: R47H variant: OR 2-4 for late-onset AD across European cohorts (Jonsson et al. 2013, Guerreiro et al. 2013). Less consistent in Asian and African populations. Other rare variants (R62H, D87N, T96K) show smaller effects. Risk after APOE4 (OR 3-12) but ahead of all other common variants.

provenance.title: Gratuze et al., 2023, Nat Rev Neurol — TREM2 review

annotations.text: TREM2-dependent and TREM2-independent microglial activation pathways operate in parallel; a TREM2 agonist would only restore the dependent arm, not full microglial function. The single on/off-switch model of TREM2 is wrong.
annotations.author: reviewer:will-blair

assertion.text: ApoE4 increases Alzheimer's risk 3-12x depending on copy number

conditions.text: Original 42-family case-control series. Heterozygous carriers (e3/e4) ~3x risk; homozygous (e4/e4) ~12x risk; e2 protective (~0.6x). Effect attenuates after age 80. Stronger in European-ancestry cohorts; weaker but significant across most ancestries; African-American cohorts show smaller effect sizes (Reiman et al. 2020).

provenance.title: Corder et al., 1993, Science 261:921-923; subsequent meta-analyses (Farrer 1997)

assertion.text: ApoE Christchurch variant may be protective even with PSEN1 mutations

conditions.text: Single Colombian woman, homozygous APOE3-Christchurch (R136S), carrying PSEN1 E280A familial AD mutation. Expected age of cognitive decline ~44; remained cognitively intact until ~70 despite extensive amyloid burden. N=1 case report; mechanism candidate: reduced ApoE binding to HSPGs limiting tau spread. Replication in additional Christchurch carriers pending.

provenance.title: Arboleda-Velasquez et al., 2019, Nature Medicine 25:1680-1683

links.note: Christchurch case shows ApoE variation can blunt risk in directions unsuspected by the simple ApoE4 risk model

assertion.text: Lecanemab showed modest cognitive benefit in Clarity AD; durability beyond 18 months and ARIA-E risk in ApoE4 homozygotes shape the real-world decision

conditions.text: Patients with mild cognitive impairment or mild AD dementia, ages 50-90, with confirmed amyloid PET positivity. Phase 3 RCT, 1795 patients, 18 months, biweekly IV infusion at 10 mg/kg. CDR-SB primary endpoint (mean difference -0.45 vs placebo, p<0.001). ARIA-E rate 12.6% overall, 32.6% in ApoE4 homozygotes; durability of cognitive benefit beyond 18 months not established by Clarity AD itself.

provenance.title: van Dyck et al., 2023, NEJM 388:9-21 (Clarity AD); subsequent open-label extension and post-marketing data

links.note: Supersedes vf_b1f04d00abcd7476 via finding.supersede proposal vpr_f988b1a6f4151cb0.

sources.title: Gratuze et al., 2023, Nat Rev Neurol — TREM2 review
van Dyck et al., 2023, NEJM 388:9-21 (Clarity AD)
Corder et al., 1993, Science 261:921-923; subsequent meta-analyses (Farrer 1997)
Egan et al., 2019, NEJM 380:1408-1420 (EPOCH verubecestat trial)
van Dyck et al., 2023, NEJM 388:9-21 (Clarity AD); subsequent open-label extension and post-marketing data

condition_records.text: Patients with mild cognitive impairment or mild AD dementia, ages 50-90, with confirmed amyloid PET positivity. ARIA-E rate 12.6% overall, 32.6% in ApoE4 homozygotes; durability of cognitive benefit beyond 18 months not established by Clarity AD itself.

Original 42-family case-control series. Heterozygous carriers (e3/e4) ~3x risk; homozygous (e4/e4) ~12x risk; e2 protective (~0.6x). Effect attenuates after age 80. Stronger in European-ancestry cohorts; weaker but significant across most ancestries; African-American cohorts show smaller effect sizes (Reiman et al. 2020).

R47H variant: OR 2-4 for late-onset AD across European cohorts (Jonsson et al. 2013, Guerreiro et al. 2013). Less consistent in Asian and African populations. Other rare variants (R62H, D87N, T96K) show smaller effects. Risk after APOE4 (OR 3-12) but ahead of all other common variants.
